From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [OryCon](/w/index.php?title=OryCon&redirect=no "OryCon"))

For the Australian scholarship programme, see [Endeavour Awards](/wiki/Endeavour_Awards "Endeavour Awards").

American literary award

The **Endeavour Award**, announced annually at **OryCon** in Portland, Oregon, is awarded to a distinguished [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") or [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy") book written by an author or authors from the [Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest") (Oregon, Washington, Idaho, British Columbia, The Yukon, and Alaska) and published in the previous year.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Annual presentation of the Endeavour Award is in November at OryCon for books published during the previous year.[[2]](#cite_note-OSFCIEAH-2)

## OryCon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endeavour_Award&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: OryCon")]

**OryCon** is an annual [science fiction/fantasy convention](/wiki/Science_fiction_convention "Science fiction convention"), held annually in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon") since 1979. OryCon's parent organization, Oregon Science Fiction Conventions, Inc. ("OSFCI") has announced plans to end support for the event after OryCon 45, to be held in October 2025.[[3]](#cite_note-DontFearTheReaper-3)

On November 11, 1978, the Portland Science Fiction Society and [Portland State University](/wiki/Portland_State_University "Portland State University") English department presented a one-day, seven-hour "Science Fiction Symposium" at the [Smith Memorial Student Union](/wiki/Smith_Memorial_Student_Union "Smith Memorial Student Union").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The organizers later established OSFCI, and presented the first OryCon the following year.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The convention canceled its 2020 event due to [COVID-19 restrictions](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States"), returning in 2021, and declared a "hiatus" for future events, citing staff burnout and the challenges of pandemic-era planning.[[6]](#cite_note-6) 2022 instead featured a free "Or-E-Con 2" virtual conference.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Award history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endeavour_Award&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Award history")]

The Endeavour Award, named for [HM Bark *Endeavour*](/wiki/HM_Bark_Endeavour "HM Bark Endeavour"), the ship of Northwest explorer [Captain James Cook](/wiki/Captain_James_Cook "Captain James Cook"), was first presented in 1999.

It was funded by a collaboration of Portland, Oregon area writers and readers of science fiction and fantasy in 1996 and chartered by Oregon Science Fiction Conventions, Inc. (OSFCI) tax-exempt non-profit corporation.

### 2020 Endeavour Award

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endeavour_Award&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2020 Endeavour Award")]

[Matt Hughes](/wiki/Matt_Hughes_(writer) "Matt Hughes (writer)") became the first Canadian winner of the Endeavour Award, for his 2019 [slipstream](/wiki/Slipstream_fiction "Slipstream fiction") [historical novel](/wiki/Historical_fiction "Historical fiction") *What the Wind Brings*.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### 2021 Endeavour Award

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endeavour_Award&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2021 Endeavour Award")]

The Endeavour Award committee announced suspension of the 2021 Endeavour Award for books published in 2020, citing [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") restrictions.[[9]](#cite_note-locus2021-01-05-9) The award was revisited and a winner announced at [Norwescon](/wiki/Norwescon "Norwescon") 45, held in SeaTac, Washington, on April 6–9, 2023.[[10]](#cite_note-locus2021-04-10-10)

### 2022 Endeavour Award

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endeavour_Award&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2022 Endeavour Award")]

The 2022 Endeavour Award was announced at [OryCon](/wiki/OryCon "OryCon") 43, held in Portland, Oregon, November 10–12, 2023.[[11]](#cite_note-locus2022-11-13-11)
